title: Spell Checking
subtitle: Built-in spell checker with per-language dictionaries.
summary: Vim ships with a real spell checker. Toggle it with `:set spell`, jump misspellings with [s/]s, suggest fixes with z=, add words to a personal dictionary with zg.

blocks.text: Vim has a real spell checker built in — not an after-thought, not a plugin. Turn it on with `:set` {key:s}{key:p}{key:e}{key:l}{key:l}, and misspelled words get a wavy underline (or whatever your `'spell'` highlight defines).

rows: Turn spell checking on for the current buffer
Spell check against multiple languages at once

sequence.note: Jump to the next misspelled word
Jump to the previous misspelled word
Same as ]s but only flag bad words (skip rare/regional)
Same as [s but only flag bad words
Show suggestions for the word under cursor (numbered list, type a digit + Enter)
Good word — add to your personal dictionary (file at 'spellfile')
Wrong word — mark as misspelled in your personal dictionary

blocks.title: Spell as window-layer state
blocks.text: Spell checking lives in the same `z`-prefixed family as folds and viewport scroll because, internally, it's the same kind of thing: presentation metadata layered over the buffer. The spell checker doesn't modify text — it asks the highlighter to mark certain ranges. The misspelling list is recomputed lazily as the window scrolls, against the merged dictionary set in `'spelllang'`. Adding a word with {key:z}{key:g} writes to your personal `'spellfile'` (default `~/.config/nvim/spell/<lang>.utf-8.add`) and triggers a recompile of the binary spell file, then re-runs the highlighter over visible lines.

blocks.title: Why `z=` opens a numbered prompt
blocks.text: The suggester is a small ranked list — typically 25 candidates by default — produced by combining edit distance, soundalike rules from the language's affix file, and case-fold rules. Vim renders the list as a numbered menu rather than as a popup because the command-line is the only piece of UI that's guaranteed to exist in every Vim build. Picking a number performs a `:s/<bad>/<good>/g` on the visible match — same substitute machinery as everything else.

Set `:set` {key:s}{key:p}{key:e}{key:l}{key:l}{key:c}{key:a}{key:p}{key:i}{key:t}{key:a}{key:l}{key:=} to disable the 'sentences must start with a capital' rule, which fires constantly inside code comments.
